insights: Ed Gompf manages 76% of IT department employees (76 out of 100), while Mariano Mauray manages only 24%, creating a severe span-of-control imbalance that exceeds recommended benchmarks (5–15 reports per manager) for both individuals.

Both IT managers exceed the upper bound of healthy span of control—Ed Gompf by 406% (76 vs. max 15) and Mariano Mauray by 60% (24 vs. max 15)—indicating systemic management overload rather than isolated overburdening.

The 76/24 workload split is not explained by role distribution, as Analyst, Developer, and Employee proportions are broadly similar under both managers (Ed: 33/32/35% vs. Mariano: 38/42/21%), ruling out functional concentration as a driver.

Geographic coverage partially overlaps—both managers supervise employees in London and Tokyo—but Ed Gompf covers 31 exclusive locations versus Mariano’s 10, indicating no clean territorial segmentation to justify the imbalance.

Work schedule distribution shows negligible concentration (HHI = 0.014) and overlapping date ranges across both managers, confirming that temporal assignment patterns do not contribute meaningfully to the workload disparity.

Neither manager appears as an employee within the IT department, eliminating internal promotion or hierarchical nesting as structural explanations—and confirming the two-manager structure is externally imposed with no built-in balancing mechanism.
summary: The IT department exhibits a severe and unexplained managerial imbalance: Ed Gompf oversees 76% of staff (76 employees), while Mariano Mauray oversees just 24% (24 employees), with both far exceeding the recommended 5–15 span-of-control range—posing clear risks of oversight gaps, delayed decision-making, and burnout. This imbalance is not attributable to differences in role composition, geographic coverage, or scheduling patterns, nor is it mitigated by internal hierarchy, as neither manager is part of the IT employee cohort. The lack of organizational logic behind the 76/24 split suggests an arbitrary or legacy assignment rather than a design aligned with operational scale or complexity. Immediate attention should focus on workload redistribution feasibility, root-cause review of assignment rationale, and assessment of whether a third manager is operationally warranted to restore sustainable governance capacity.
